title: Najdorf Sicilian
summary: The sharpest opening in modern chess

steps.title: Fischer's Favorite
steps.text:
The **Najdorf Variation** of the Sicilian Defense is widely considered the **sharpest, most theoretical, most studied** opening in chess history. It was the favorite weapon of **Bobby Fischer** and **Garry Kasparov** — two of the most aggressive world champions ever — and remains a staple at the highest levels.

## How you reach the Najdorf

The defining sequence is {{e4}} **{{c5}}** {{Nf3}} **{{d6}}** {{d4}} **{{cxd4}}** {{Nxd4}} **{{Nf6}}** {{Nc3}} **a6** — and you're in. The little move **5...a6** is the soul of the variation.

## Why does **5...a6** matter?

- **Prevents Bb5+ pin/check ideas** that could disrupt Black's development.
- **Prepares ...e5** by removing the b5 square as a potential outpost for White's pieces.
- **Prepares ...b5** — Black's queenside pawn storm.
- **Stays flexible** — Black hasn't committed to a piece setup yet, leaving White to choose first.

## What each side wants

- **White's plan:** choose an attacking setup (English Attack, Bg5, Be2, Bc4) and aim for a kingside or central breakthrough. Castle long if possible (English Attack), then **g4-g5** to crash the kingside.
- **Black's plan:** develop with **...e5** or **...e6**, **...Be7** or **...Nbd7**, **...b5**, and counter-attack on the queenside. The Najdorf is famous for *opposite-side castling races*.
- **Pawn structure:** characteristic Najdorf chains — Black at d6/e6/a6/b5 vs White at e4/f3 (English Attack) or e4/f4 (Be2 lines).

> The Najdorf is chess at its most violent. Both kings under fire, every move a calculation, no quiet middlegames.

steps.title: Main Lines
steps.text:
On move 6, White faces an embarrassment of riches — *six* serious tries, each leading to a distinct middlegame.

## The big six White moves

- **6.Be3** — *English Attack*. White plays {{Be3}}, **f3**, **Qd2**, **0-0-0**, then **g2-g4-g5**. Mutual attacks on opposite sides. The most popular modern line.
- **6.Bg5** — *Classical Najdorf*. The bishop pins f6 and prepares to trade. Leads to wild theory in the **Poisoned Pawn** and **Goteborg** variations.
- **6.Bc4** — *Fischer-Sozin Attack*. Bobby Fischer's pet line. Bishop targets f7; White goes for direct kingside fireworks.
- **6.Be2** — *Classical / Opocensky*. Quiet, solid, modest goals. Often transposes into Scheveningen-style middlegames.
- **6.f3** — preparing the English Attack one move earlier.
- **6.h3** — modern fashion, popularized by **Magnus Carlsen** and **Wesley So**. Prepares **g4** without **f3** weaknesses.

## Choosing your weapon

- *Want maximum sharpness?* — English Attack with 6.Be3.
- *Want pure aggression?* — Bg5 Poisoned Pawn or Bc4 Fischer-Sozin.
- *Want positional play?* — 6.Be2 or 6.h3.

## The Najdorf cost

Learning the Najdorf is a **lifetime project**. Theory runs 20+ moves deep in many lines. Books on just one variation can run hundreds of pages.

> The Najdorf rewards study like no other opening. Investment goes in — wins come out.

steps.title: The Poisoned Pawn
steps.text:
After **6.Bg5 e6 7.f4**, Black faces a fork in the road. The famously poisoned reply is **7...Qb6!?** — daring White to defend the **b2** pawn.

## Why "poisoned"?

Because if Black greedily eats the pawn with **8...Qxb2**, White abandons the b-file in exchange for an enormous attack: **9.Rb1 Qa3** and now White has **Rb3** ideas, **e5 break**, **Bxf6 + Bd3**, and a roaring center.

## The Poisoned Pawn historic line

The critical line goes **6.Bg5 e6 7.f4 Qb6 8.Qd2 Qxb2 9.Rb1 Qa3 10.f5** — and now Black has to navigate a thicket of threats with the queen out of play. Spassky used this line against Fischer in the 1972 World Championship.

## What does each side gamble?

- **Black gambles:** the queen on a3 is exposed, and the queenside structure is shattered. But if White's attack fizzles, Black's extra pawn and bishop pair can decide the endgame.
- **White gambles:** the b2 pawn is gone, but White's pieces are screaming forward and Black's king is still in the center.

## Why study it

The Poisoned Pawn is *the* example of a modern computer-tested gambit — engines confirm Black is fine **with perfect play**, but the human margin for error is razor-thin. Both sides need 20+ moves of theory to survive.

> Greedy moves can win games — if you're prepared to walk a tightrope to defend them.
